0.description: A fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis that affects the leaves and fruit of apple trees, resulting in olive-green to black velvety lesions.
symptoms: Dark, scabby lesions on leaves and fruit
Premature leaf drop
treatment: Apply fungicides like captan or myclobutanil
prevention: Prune trees for better air circulation
0.management_tips: Monitor trees during wet seasons and apply fungicide as needed.
0.localized_tips: Ensure proper pruning to reduce humidity

1.description: Caused by the fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa, black rot affects the fruit, leaves, and bark of apple trees.
symptoms: Circular brown lesions on fruit
Purple-bordered leaf spots
treatment: Remove and destroy infected branches and fruit
1.management_tips: Clean up mummified fruits and dead wood from the orchard.
1.localized_tips: Remove mummified fruit and prune affected branches

2.description: A fungal disease requiring both apple and cedar (juniper) hosts to complete its lifecycle.
symptoms: Bright orange spots on leaves
Galls on cedar trees
treatment: Remove nearby cedar trees if possible
2.management_tips: Monitor both apple and cedar hosts for symptoms and apply fungicide preventively.

3.management_tips: Regular inspection and proper irrigation help maintain plant health.
3.localized_tips: Keep foliage dry by watering at the base

4.management_tips: Regularly inspect bushes and provide adequate watering and mulching.
4.sprayer_intervals: Preventive fungicide once per month if necessary
4.localized_tips: Water at the base and avoid overhead irrigation

5.description: A fungal disease caused by Podosphaera clandestina, affecting leaves, shoots, and fruits with white powdery growth.
symptoms: White powdery spots on leaves and young shoots
Curling or distortion of leaves
Reduced fruit quality
treatment: Apply fungicides such as sulfur or myclobutanil
prevention: Ensure good air circulation through pruning
5.management_tips: Monitor during dry, warm weather and treat at first signs of infection.
5.localized_tips: Avoid planting in shaded, poorly ventilated areas

6.management_tips: Monitor regularly and provide balanced fertilization for tree vigor.
6.sprayer_intervals: Preventive fungicide every 3-4 weeks if needed
6.localized_tips: Ensure trees are spaced properly for air flow

7.description: A fungal disease caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis, which causes rectangular lesions on corn leaves.
symptoms: Long, narrow, rectangular gray to tan lesions on leaves
Leaf tissue death and premature blight
treatment: Apply fungicides such as strobilurins or triazoles
Use crop rotation to reduce residue-borne inoculum
prevention: Rotate crops and manage crop residue
7.management_tips: Scout fields early and apply fungicides when necessary to prevent yield loss.
7.sprayer_intervals: Every 7-10 days if conditions favor infection
7.localized_tips: Avoid continuous corn planting and use no-till residue management

8.description: A fungal disease caused by Puccinia sorghi, forming reddish-brown pustules on leaves.
symptoms: Small, round, reddish-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces
Yellowing and premature leaf death
treatment: Use fungicides like azoxystrobin or propiconazole
8.management_tips: Early detection is key; apply fungicides at first signs.
8.sprayer_intervals: Every 10–14 days during active rust periods

9.description: Caused by Exserohilum turcicum, this disease leads to cigar-shaped lesions and premature leaf death.
symptoms: Long, elliptical gray-green lesions on leaves
Leaf blighting and reduced grain fill
treatment: Apply fungicides like pyraclostrobin or propiconazole
9.management_tips: Monitor weather and leaf conditions during humid periods.
9.localized_tips: Use proper tillage to reduce infected debris

prevention: Use good agricultural practices and resistant hybrids
10.management_tips: Inspect regularly and ensure balanced nutrition and watering.
10.sprayer_intervals: Preventive spray once per month if needed
10.localized_tips: Avoid crowding and ensure proper spacing

11.description: A fungal disease caused by Guignardia bidwellii that affects all green parts of the grapevine.
symptoms: Small black spots on leaves and stems
Fruit mummification and shriveling
treatment: Apply fungicides like mancozeb or myclobutanil
Remove and destroy infected plant parts
11.management_tips: Remove mummified berries and infected leaves promptly.
11.sprayer_intervals: Every 7–10 days during bloom and fruit development
11.localized_tips: Use drip irrigation to avoid wet foliage

12.description: A complex fungal disease involving multiple pathogens including Phaeomoniella and Phaeoacremonium species.
symptoms: Interveinal leaf chlorosis and necrosis
Black spots or 'measles' on fruit
Sudden vine collapse in late summer
treatment: No effective chemical treatment; remove affected vines
12.management_tips: Avoid pruning wounds and maintain vine health.
12.localized_tips: Discard infected wood and apply wound protectants

13.description: Caused by the fungus Isariopsis clavispora, resulting in necrotic leaf spots and defoliation.
symptoms: Angular brown or black spots on leaves
Leaf yellowing and premature drop
treatment: Apply fungicides like mancozeb or copper-based sprays
13.management_tips: Apply treatments before symptoms become severe.

14.management_tips: Use fungicide preventively during wet seasons.
14.sprayer_intervals: Preventive fungicide every 3–4 weeks if necessary
14.localized_tips: Monitor canopy humidity and avoid overhead watering

15.description: A bacterial disease caused by Candidatus Liberibacter spp., spread by the Asian citrus psyllid.
symptoms: Yellow shoots and blotchy leaf mottling
Small, lopsided, bitter fruits
Tree decline and dieback
treatment: No cure; remove and destroy infected trees
15.management_tips: Monitor psyllid populations and report suspicious trees.
15.sprayer_intervals: Insecticides every 10–14 days in active vector areas
15.localized_tips: Use screenhouses or barriers for young trees

16.description: A bacterial disease caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. pruni, affecting leaves, fruit, and twigs.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn dark and angular
Sunken lesions on fruit leading to cracking
Twig dieback in severe infections
16.management_tips: Monitor after rain events and apply copper sprays early in the season.
16.localized_tips: Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce leaf wetness

17.management_tips: Ensure balanced fertilization and regular monitoring.
17.sprayer_intervals: Preventive spray once per month if needed
17.localized_tips: Water at ground level and mulch to conserve moisture

18.description: Caused by Xanthomonas euvesicatoria, this bacterial disease forms spots on leaves and fruit.
symptoms: Circular to irregular water-soaked spots on leaves
Lesions on fruit causing scabby, cratered spots
prevention: Avoid working when foliage is wet
18.management_tips: Begin sprays prior to flowering and continue through fruit set.
18.sprayer_intervals: Every 7–10 days during warm, wet periods
18.localized_tips: Mulch plants and improve air circulation

prevention: Provide balanced nutrients and keep soil moist but not wet
19.management_tips: Regular scouting and ensure good drainage.
19.sprayer_intervals: Preventive treatments every 3–4 weeks if needed
19.localized_tips: Avoid crowding and apply mulch to stabilize soil moisture

20.description: Fungal disease caused by Alternaria solani, infecting leaves, stems, and tubers.
symptoms: Dark brown to black concentric ring spots on leaves
Lesions on stems and tubers
treatment: Apply fungicides like chlorothalonil or azoxystrobin
Remove volunteer potato plants and infected debris
20.management_tips: Start sprays when foliage is 10–15 cm tall and repeat on a 7–10 day schedule.
20.localized_tips: Mulch to reduce soil splash and moisture on leaves

21.description: Caused by Phytophthora infestans, leads to rapid plant collapse under cool, wet conditions.
symptoms: Dark, water-soaked lesions on leaves and stems
White fungal growth under lesions in humid conditions
Rotting tubers
treatment: Apply fungicides such as mancozeb or metalaxyl
Remove and destroy infected foliage immediately
21.management_tips: Begin protective sprays when conditions are favorable; reapply every 5–7 days.
21.sprayer_intervals: Every 5–7 days in cool wet weather
21.localized_tips: Avoid overhead watering and space hills for airflow

prevention: Practice crop rotation and use certified seed
22.management_tips: Ensure well‑drained soil and regular pest scouting.
22.sprayer_intervals: Preventive fungicide once per month if needed
22.localized_tips: Avoid planting in low areas prone to pooling water

prevention: Maintain proper pruning, fertilization, and site sanitation
23.management_tips: Ensure good air circulation, proper mulching, and monitor for pests.
23.sprayer_intervals: Preventive treatments once per month, if needed
23.localized_tips: Remove old canes and provide regular irrigation at the root zone

prevention: Rotate crops, monitor pests and nutrient levels
24.management_tips: Scout fields regularly and ensure balanced soil fertility.
24.sprayer_intervals: Preventive fungicide once per month under wet conditions
24.localized_tips: Ensure proper drainage and reduce plant density if necessary

25.description: A fungal disease caused by Podosphaera xanthii or Erysiphe cichoracearum, forming white powdery growth on leaves and stems.
symptoms: White to gray powdery patches on leaf surfaces
Leaf yellowing, curling, and premature defoliation
treatment: Apply fungicides like sulfur, myclobutanil, or potassium bicarbonate
Remove infected leaves when disease starts
prevention: Ensure good airflow and avoid overhead watering
25.management_tips: Treat at first sign of powdery growth and repeat every 7–10 days.
25.localized_tips: Space plants widely and mulch to reduce humidity

26.description: A fungal disease caused by Diplocarpon earlianum, resulting in brown lesions and leaf edge necrosis.
symptoms: Circular to irregular brown lesions on leaves
Leaf edges turn brown and scorched, leading to defoliation
treatment: Apply fungicides such as captan or chlorothalonil
prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation and ensure good plant spacing
26.management_tips: Spray preventively during warm, wet weather and remove debris post-harvest.
26.sprayer_intervals: Every 7–14 days when conditions remain favorable
26.localized_tips: Space beds properly and use drip irrigation

prevention: Practice crop rotation and mulching to maintain plant health
27.management_tips: Monitor regularly for pests and diseases; maintain optimal soil moisture.
27.sprayer_intervals: Preventive spray once per month if needed
27.localized_tips: Remove dying leaves and monitor plant vigor

28.description: A bacterial disease caused by Xanthomonas spp., producing small, water‑soaked leaf and fruit lesions.
symptoms: Small (≤1/8″), circular brown spots on leaves, often water‑soaked
Raised scabby lesions on green and ripe fruit
prevention: Rotate crops and remove infected debris
28.management_tips: Begin sprays early in warm, humid weather and maintain sanitation :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}.
28.sprayer_intervals: Every 7–10 days during warm, wet weather
28.localized_tips: Use drip irrigation and ensure plant spacing

29.description: Fungal disease caused by Alternaria solani, with concentric ring lesions on leaves, stems, and fruit.
symptoms: Brown leaf spots with ‘bull’s‑eye’ concentric rings
Stem lesions and fruit rot
treatment: Remove volunteer potatoes and affected debris
29.management_tips: Spray when plants reach 10–15 cm and repeat every 7–10 days in humid conditions.

30.description: A serious fungal disease caused by Phytophthora infestans, affecting leaves, stems, and fruit.
symptoms: Large, dark brown blotches with green halos on leaves
Fruits develop firm, dark lesions
treatment: Apply fungicide like chlorothalonil or mancozeb
30.management_tips: Avoid overhead watering to reduce moisture on leaves.
30.localized_tips: Improve drainage and spacing to reduce humidity

31.description: Fungal disease by Passalora fulva, causing olive‑green velvety growth on leaf undersides.
symptoms: Pale green/yellow spots on upper leaves
Olive‑green velvety sporulation on leaf undersides
31.management_tips: Control humidity in protected production settings :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}.
31.localized_tips: Use drip irrigation and space plants wide

32.description: A fungal disease by Septoria lycopersici; causes small leaf spots with black fruiting bodies.
symptoms: Circular brown lesions (2–5 mm) with gray centers
Tiny black pycnidia in the center of spots
32.sprayer_intervals: Every 7–14 days when humidity is high
32.localized_tips: Remove lower leaves to improve airflow

33.description: Stressed plants attacked by Tetranychus urticae, causing speckled foliage and webbing.
symptoms: Yellow stippling on leaves
Fine webbing on leaf undersides and stems
treatment: Rinse plants with strong water spray
33.management_tips: Frequent inspections and early control are essential.
33.localized_tips: Avoid excessive nitrogen, which attracts mites

34.description: Fungal disease by Corynespora cassiicola, producing circular leaf spots often with concentric rings.
symptoms: Circular to irregular spots with yellow halos
Lesions may have target‑like rings
34.management_tips: Treat early in disease cycle and reduce humidity.

35.description: A geminivirus spread by whiteflies, causing leaf yellowing, curling, and stunting.
symptoms: Upward rolling and yellowing of young leaves
Stunted plant growth
35.management_tips: Monitor whiteflies and use reflective mulches.
35.sprayer_intervals: Apply insecticide every 7–14 days during outbreaks
35.localized_tips: Use screened greenhouses in high‑pressure zones

36.description: A Tobamovirus spread mechanically and via contaminated seed, causing mottling and stunted fruit.
symptoms: Mosaic leaf discoloration
Leaf distortion and stunted growth
36.management_tips: Implement strict hygiene and resistant varieties.
36.localized_tips: Wear gloves and disinfect between handling plants

37.management_tips: Monitor regularly and use balanced fertilization.
37.sprayer_intervals: Preventive fungicide once per month if needed
